intent: Report the shutdown schedule as guarded rather than skipped, now that guard-file@shutdown-schedule.path is confirmed active.
scope: Non-goal: system state — the legacy units were disabled by the user, not by this change
changes: Test 10 now accepts guard-file@shutdown-schedule.path, the guard-lib replacement that actually protects /etc/shutdown-schedule.conf today. Previously it only knew the retired shutdown-schedule-guard.path and reported a skip, which understated the real protection. The disabled-is-intentional branch is kept for hosts that have neither unit.

verification.evidence: 19 passed / 0 failed / 5 skipped, up from 18/0/6: 'shutdown schedule is guarded' now PASSes instead of skipping. /etc/shutdown-schedule.conf carries the immutable flag and guard-file@shutdown-schedule.path is active.

risks: Same pattern as the other guard checks: if the unit is renamed again, this reports a false skip rather than a failure. The disabled-is-intentional branch is what makes that possible, and it is the deliberate trade for not failing on a host that legitimately retired the guard.
rollback: git revert this commit; Test 10 returns to skipping on this host.
Validate after rollback: the suite reports 18 passed / 6 skipped with 'shutdown-schedule-guard.path is active' skipped.
